reflection.quote: We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit.
reflection.reading:
Monday morning in mid-May. Nothing particularly special about it. No crisis, no celebration, just a week opening up in front of you with its emails and errands and the same coffee you made yesterday. This is the part of sobriety nobody warned me about, and it turned out to be the most important part.

When I was drinking, ordinary Mondays were the enemy. They were the long flat stretch between weekends, something to be survived or numbed or escaped. The whole architecture of my week was built around getting away from regular life. What I didn't understand at 22 was that regular life is most of life. If you can't stand a Tuesday afternoon, you can't stand being alive, and no amount of weekend will fix that. Sobriety didn't hand me a perpetual ski trip. It handed me back the boring middle of the week, and somehow that turned out to be the gift.

Durant's line about excellence as a habit isn't really about excellence. It's about identity. You become the person who shows up on a Monday in May when nothing's on fire. You answer the email. You go for the walk. You call your mom back. None of it photographs well. All of it is the actual material your life is made of. Today is not a small day. Today is the day.
reflection.meditation: Where can I show up fully in the ordinary parts of today?

affirmations: I am building a life one ordinary day at a time, and today counts.
Today I choose presence over escape, even when nothing dramatic is happening.
I am the person who shows up — for the small things, the boring things, and myself.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful for a clear head on a Monday morning, for the unremarkable gift of waking up without dread or regret. Grateful for the people who answer when I call, for work to do, for a body that moves, for the quiet privilege of a week ahead I get to actually be present for. Eleven years ago this was unimaginable. Today it's just Monday — and that's the whole miracle.
